quizSrc.term: Company XYZ provides consulting services to Company ABC in November 2022 for a fee of $100,000. Company ABC pays $50,000 in cash in November 2022 and the remaining $50,000 in January 2023. Company XYZ incurred $10,000 in expenses related to the consulting services in November 2022, which will be paid in December 2022. Additionally, on November 1, 2022, Company XYZ pre-paid $12,000 for rent for the next twelve months. What should Company XYZ recognize as net income/ net operating cash flow for November 2022? A. Net Income: $89,000; Net Operating Cash Flow: $50,000. B. Net Income: $88,000; Net Operating Cash Flow: $48,000. C. Net Income: $89,000; Net Operating Cash Flow: $38,000. D. Net Income: $100,000, Net Operating Cash Flow: $50,000.
quizSrc.definition: C. Net Income: $89,000; Net Operating Cash Flow: $38,000.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following is NOT an objective of the Conceptual Framework, as defined by US GAAP? A. To provide a framework for standard-setting B. To enhance consistency and comparability in financial reporting C. To assist preparers of financial statements in applying US GAAP D. To establish specific rules for recognizing and measuring transactions and events
quizSrc.definition: D. To establish specific rules for recognizing and measuring transactions and events

quizSrc.term: Which factors would raise doubts about an entity's ability to continue as a going concern under US GAAP? A. A history of profitable operations and strong cash flows. B. A significant decrease in demand for the entity's products or services. C. A stable industry with no significant competitive pressures. D. A strong balance sheet with high levels of liquidity and low levels of debt.
quizSrc.definition: B. A significant decrease in demand for the entity's products or services.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following statements regarding the use of IFRS in the United States is true? A. All US companies must use IFRS for financial reporting purposes. B. Only foreign subsidiaries of US companies are allowed to use IFRS. C. The US has fully adopted IFRS and no longer requires the use of US GAAP. D. US companies can voluntarily choose to use IFRS for financial reporting purposes.
quizSrc.definition: B. Only foreign subsidiaries of US companies are allowed to use IFRS.

quizSrc.term: Which organization is primarily responsible for establishing and improving US Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)? A. The Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) B. The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) C. The International Accounting Standards Board (IASB) D. The American Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA)
quizSrc.definition: A. The Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB)

quizSrc.term: Which of the following best describes the principle of verifiability in financial reporting? A. Financial statements should provide relevant information that is important to users B. Financial statements should include information that can be verified or confirmed through independent sources or methods C. Financial statements should provide a clear and concise representation of the company's financial position, performance, and cash flows D. Financial statements should be prepared in a way that is easily understandable to users with a basic knowledge of business and accounting
quizSrc.definition: B. Financial statements should include information that can be verified or confirmed through independent sources or methods

quizSrc.term: Which of the following is an example of the historical cost principle in accounting? A. Recording a machine on the balance sheet at its original purchase price B. Recording the fair value of a stock investment on the balance sheet C. Recording the value of inventory at its current market price D. Recording the estimated market value of a company's trademarks on the balance sheet
quizSrc.definition: A. Recording a machine on the balance sheet at its original purchase price

quizSrc.term: Which financial statement provides information about a company's revenues, expenses, and net income or loss over a period of time? A. Income statement B. Statement of cash flows C. Statement of Stockholders’ equity D. Balance sheet

Which of the following is an example of accrual accounting? A. A company records revenue only when it receives cash payments from customers. B. A company records revenue when it delivers products or services to customers, regardless of when it receives payment. C. A company records expenses when it receives bills from suppliers. D. A company records expenses when it pays cash for goods and services.
quizSrc.definition: B. A company records revenue when it delivers products or services to customers, regardless of when it receives payment.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following scenarios demonstrates the use of accrual accounting? A. A business records a $10,000 sale on credit on December 31st, but the payment is not received until January 15th of the following year. B. A business purchases $5,000 worth of inventory on credit on December 31st, but pays for it in cash on January 5th of the following year. C. A business records a $3,000 cash sale on December 31st and deposits the cash on the same day. D. A business records a $2,000 expense for rent on December 31st, but the payment is not due until February 1st of the following year.
quizSrc.definition: A. A business records a $10,000 sale on credit on December 31st, but the payment is not received until January 15th of the following year.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following is an example of an accounting process? A. Researching new marketing strategies for a business B. Calculating payroll taxes for employees C. Ordering inventory for a retail store D. Posting a job opening on a company website
quizSrc.definition: B. Calculating payroll taxes for employees

quizSrc.term: Which of the following best describes a T-account? A. A record of all financial transactions in a business B. A summary of all accounts in the general ledger C. A graphical representation of debits and credits for a specific account D. A statement of financial position of a company at a specific point in time
quizSrc.definition: C. A graphical representation of debits and credits for a specific account

quizSrc.term: What is the purpose of an adjusting entry in the accounting process? A. To record transactions that have not yet been posted B. To update accounts to their proper amounts for financial reporting C. To correct errors in the general ledger D. To close out temporary accounts at the end of the accounting period
quizSrc.definition: B. To update accounts to their proper amounts for financial reporting

quizSrc.term: Company X's retained earnings balance at the end of 2024 was $60,000. During that year, the company paid out $10,000 in dividends and had a net income of $25,000. What was the company's retained earnings balance at the beginning of 2024? A. $45,000 B. $55,000 C. $75,000 D. $95,000

If a company borrows money from a bank, how does this transaction affect the company's liabilities and assets? A. Liabilities and assets both decrease B. Liabilities increase and assets decrease C. Liabilities and assets both increase D. Liabilities decrease and assets increase
quizSrc.definition: C. Liabilities and assets both increase

quizSrc.term: Which of the following best describes the revenue recognition concept in accounting? A. Recognizing revenue when the product is shipped to the customer B. Recognizing revenue when the customer places an order C. Recognizing revenue when cash is received D. Recognizing revenue when it is earned, regardless of when payment is received
quizSrc.definition: D. Recognizing revenue when it is earned, regardless of when payment is received

quizSrc.term: Which of the following statements best describes a non-adjusted trial balance? A. It is prepared before any adjusting entries are made B. It is the final step in the accounting cycle C. It includes only the ending balances of all accounts D. It includes all transactions that occurred during the accounting period
quizSrc.definition: A. It is prepared before any adjusting entries are made

quizSrc.term: Which of the following statements is true about closing entries in accounting? A. Closing entries are made at the beginning of the accounting period to record opening balances. B. Closing entries are used to close permanent accounts only. C. Closing entries transfer net income or loss to the retained earnings account. D. Closing entries are optional and unnecessary for preparing financial statements.
quizSrc.definition: C. Closing entries transfer net income or loss to the retained earnings account.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following is an example of an item that may be included in the statement of comprehensive income? A. Dividends paid to shareholders B. Interest income earned on investments C. Purchase of a new office building D. Unrealized gains in available for sale investments
quizSrc.definition: D. Unrealized gains in available for sale investments

quizSrc.term: Which of the following would be considered a permanent account? A. Prepaid expenses B. Depreciation expense C. Sales revenue D. Cost of goods sold

At the end of the 2022 fiscal year, Company X has current assets of $800,000, including inventory of $120,000 and prepaid rent of $50,000. Its total assets are $1,500,000, current liabilities are $200,000, and total liabilities are $400,000. What is Company X's current ratio? A. 2.0 B. 3.00 C. 4.00 D. 5.0

At the end of the 2022 fiscal year, Company X has current assets of $800,000, including inventory of $120,000 and prepaid rent of $50,000. Its total assets are $1,500,000, current liabilities are $200,000, and total liabilities are $400,000. What is Company X's debt to equity ratio? A. 0.27 B. 1.36 C. 2.75 D. 0.36

Which of the following best describes the times interest earned (TIE) ratio? A. It measures a company's ability to pay its short-term debts as they come due. B. It measures a company's ability to generate profits relative to its invested capital. C. It measures a company's ability to meet its interest obligations. D. It measures a company's ability to pay off all of its debts in the next fiscal year.
quizSrc.definition: C. It measures a company's ability to meet its interest obligations.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following policy decisions would not be included in the Summary of Significant Accounting Policies? A. Revenue recognition for long-term construction contracts B. Costing method for inventory C. Depreciation method D. Issuance of debt or equity
quizSrc.definition: D. Issuance of debt or equity

quizSrc.term: Given the importance of liquidity ratios in measuring a company's ability to meet its short-term obligations, which of the following transactions would contribute to an increase in a company's liquidity ratios? A. Borrow cash by signing a two-year note B. Pay dividends to shareholders C. Pay suppliers with cash D. Purchase inventories with cash
quizSrc.definition: A. Borrow cash by signing a two-year note

quizSrc.term: Which of the following statements accurately categorizes accumulated depreciation in the Balance Sheet, a financial statement that provides information about a company's financial position at a specific point in time? A. An asset category that reflects the value of an asset, such as property or equipment, after accounting for depreciation. B. A liability category that includes long-term debt obligations, such as loans or bonds. C. A contra liability category that offsets the value of a liability, such as accounts payable or notes payable. D. A contra-asset category that offsets the value of an asset, such as property or equipment, and reflects the total depreciation expense incurred over the asset's useful life.
quizSrc.definition: D. A contra-asset category that offsets the value of an asset, such as property or equipment, and reflects the total depreciation expense incurred over the asset's useful life.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following factors hinders the efficacy of the Balance Sheet for internal and external decision makers? A. The Balance Sheet reports amounts at a specific point in time instead of covering an entire period. B. The Balance Sheet is not reviewed as frequently by an independent entity as the other financial statements. C. The Balance Sheet utilizes assumptions to determine Balance Sheet values. D. The Balance Sheet doesn't provide information regarding the company's revenues and expenses.
quizSrc.definition: C. The Balance Sheet utilizes assumptions to determine Balance Sheet values.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following statements is true regarding the differences between Form 10-K, Form 10-Q, and Form 8-K? A. Form 8-K is filed annually and includes audited financial statements, while Form 10-K is filed quarterly and includes unaudited financial statements. B. Form 10-K and Form 10-Q are both filed quarterly and include audited financial statements, while Form 8-K is filed annually and includes unaudited financial statements. C. Form 10-Q is filed annually and includes audited financial statements, while Form 10-K is filed quarterly and includes unaudited financial statements. D. Form 10-K is filed annually and includes audited financial statements, while Form 10-Q is filed quarterly and includes unaudited financial statements.
quizSrc.definition: D. Form 10-K is filed annually and includes audited financial statements, while Form 10-Q is filed quarterly and includes unaudited financial statements.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following statements is true regarding the difference between unqualified and qualified audit reports? A. An unqualified audit report indicates that there were no significant deficiencies or material weaknesses found in the audit, while a qualified audit report indicates that there were some deficiencies but they were not material. B. An unqualified audit report indicates that the financial statements are free of material misstatements, while a qualified audit report indicates that there were material misstatements found in the financial statements. C. An unqualified audit report indicates that the financial statements are presented fairly in accordance with GAAP, while a qualified audit report indicates that the financial statements were not prepared in accordance with GAAP. D. An unqualified audit report indicates that the auditor is unable to express an opinion on the financial statements, while a qualified audit report indicates that the auditor has expressed an adverse opinion on the financial statements.
quizSrc.definition: C. An unqualified audit report indicates that the financial statements are presented fairly in accordance with GAAP, while a qualified audit report indicates that the financial statements were not prepared in accordance with GAAP.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following is an example of a segment as defined by the segment reporting standards? A. Product line B. Headquarters location C. Type of employee benefit plan D. Office furniture

What is the difference between a single-step income statement and a multiple-step income statement? A. A single-step income statement combines all revenues and expenses into one category, while a multiple-step income statement separates them into several categories. B. A single-step income statement is more detailed than a multiple-step income statement. C. A single-step income statement is easier to understand than a multiple-step income statement. D. A single-step income statement is used for small businesses, while a multiple-step income statement is used for large corporations.
quizSrc.definition: A. A single-step income statement combines all revenues and expenses into one category, while a multiple-step income statement separates them into several categories.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following is an example of other income (expense) on the income statement? A. Interest Revenue B. Revenue from the sale of goods or services C. Cost of goods sold D. Wages and salaries expense

What is the difference between continued operations and discontinued operations on an income statement? A. Continued operations are non-recurring business activities that occur regularly, while discontinued operations are recurring business activities that are expected to continue in the future. B. Continued operations are business activities that generate a profit, while discontinued operations are business activities that generate a loss. C. Continued operations are ongoing business activities that are expected to continue in the future, while discontinued operations are business activities that have been sold or terminated. D. Continued operations are business activities that have a significant impact on the financial statements, while discontinued operations are business activities that have little or no impact on the financial statements.
quizSrc.definition: C. Continued operations are ongoing business activities that are expected to continue in the future, while discontinued operations are business activities that have been sold or terminated.

quizSrc.term: What is the formula for calculating earnings per share (EPS)? A. Total liabilities divided by total equity B. Net income divided by total revenue C. Net income divided by total assets D. Net income minus preferred dividends divided by average common shares outstanding
quizSrc.definition: D. Net income minus preferred dividends divided by average common shares outstanding

quizSrc.term: A company has a net income of $500,000 and non-convertible preferred stock dividends of $50,000. The company has 1,000,000 shares of common stock outstanding for the entire year and also has stock options outstanding that are convertible into 100,000 shares of common stock. What is the diluted earnings per share (EPS) for the company? A. $0.41 B. $0.55 C. $0.50 D. $0.45

Which of the following is not a commonly used non-GAAP earnings metric? A. EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) B. Gross Profit Margin C. Non-Recurring Expenses D. Adjusted Earnings

Using the indirect method, what is the cash flow from operating activities based on the following information? Net income = $15,000 Loss on sale of equipment = $3,500 Increase in accounts receivable = $1,200 Decrease in inventory = $400 Increase in accounts payable = $700 A. $16,000 B. $14,200 C. $18,400 D. $13,600

What is the difference between the direct and indirect method of preparing the cash flow statement? A. The direct method reports actual cash inflows and outflows from operating activities, while the indirect method adjusts net income for non-cash items. B. The direct method reports cash inflows and outflows from operating activities, while the indirect method reports changes in balance sheet accounts. C. The direct method reports cash inflows and outflows from investing activities, while the indirect method reports changes in cash and cash equivalents. D. The direct method reports cash inflows and outflows from financing activities, while the indirect method reports changes in income and expenses.
quizSrc.definition: A. The direct method reports actual cash inflows and outflows from operating activities, while the indirect method adjusts net income for non-cash items.

quizSrc.term: A company has accounts receivable of $50,000 and credit sales of $200,000 for the year. What is the average collection period for accounts receivable? (round to the nearest number) A. 90 days B. 30 days C. 60 days D. 120 days
A company has a net profit margin of 8%, an asset turnover of 1.5, and an equity multiplier of 2. What is the return on equity (ROE)? A. 24% B. 16% C. 36% D. 12%

Emily has $5,000 that she wants to invest in a savings account that earns 3% interest compounded annually for a period of four years. What is the expected future value of her investment at the end of the four-year term? A. $5,615 B. $5,769 C. $5,627 D. $6,337

Which of the following best describes the concept of time value of money? A. Money is worth less today than the same amount in the future due to inflation. B. Money has the same value today and in the future, regardless of the timing of the cash flow. C. Money is worth more in the future than the same amount today due to the potential to earn interest. D. Money is worth more today than the same amount in the future due to inflation.
quizSrc.definition: D. Money is worth more today than the same amount in the future due to inflation.

quizSrc.term: David wants to invest $10,000 in a savings account that earns 4% simple interest per year for two years. What is the expected future value of his investment at the end of the two-year term? A. $10,400 B. $10,800 C. $11,200 D. $11,600

What is the key difference between an ordinary annuity, an annuity due, and a deferred annuity? A. The timing of the payments B. The amount of the payments C. The type of investment vehicle D. The frequency of the payments
quizSrc.definition: A. The timing of the payments

quizSrc.term: The Acme Electronics Corporation wants to accumulate $5,000,000 for research and development. The funds are needed on January 1, 2027. Acme intends to make four equal annual deposits in a fund that will earn interest at 6% compounded annually. The first deposit is to be made today, January 1, 2023. What is the amount of the required annual deposit? A. $1,250,000 B. $1,582,180 C. $1,078,262 D. $1,142,957

Carla plans to make monthly deposits of $150 for four years into a savings account. The first deposit will be made immediately. The savings account pays interest at an annual rate of 6%, compounded monthly. How much will Carla have accumulated in the savings account at the end of the four-year period? Round to the nearest dollar. A. $7,305 B. $8,115 C. $8,155 D. $9,011

On January 1, 2022, the Johnson Company purchased merchandise from a supplier. Payment was a non-interest bearing note requiring four annual payments of $30,000 on each December 31 beginning on December 31, 2022, and a lump-sum payment of $120,000 on December 31, 2025. A 12% interest rate properly reflects the time value of money in this situation. How much should you capitalize the merchandise purchase in the assets? A. $150,000 B. $155,622 C. $165,000 D. $167,383

The Kiddo Toy Company needs to acquire the use of a new piece of equipment to enhance its manufacturing process. The machine in question is manufactured by the Bouncy Corp. The machine has an estimated useful life of 15 years and can be sold for $20,000 at the end of its useful life. Bouncy Corp has provided Kiddo Toy Company with two options: Buy the Machine. The equipment can be purchased outright for $300,000. The machine's annual maintenance and insurance cost is expected to be around $10,000 per year, which Kiddo Toy Company would pay. Lease the Machine. The machine can be leased for 15 years with an annual lease payment of $35,000. The first payment is due immediately. Bouncy Corp will cover all maintenance and insurance costs. At the end of the 15-year lease period, the machine would be returned to Bouncy Corp. If the time value of money is estimated to be 10%, which option should Kiddo Toy Company choose? A. It doesn't matter as the costs are the same. B. Buy the machine, as it is cheaper in the long run. C. Insufficient information to determine the best option. D. Lease the machine, as it is cheaper in the long run.
quizSrc.definition: B. Buy the machine, as it is cheaper in the long run.

quizSrc.term: Widget Co. needs to acquire the use of a piece of equipment to manufacture its products. The equipment needed is manufactured by Gear Inc. The equipment can be used for 7 years and then sold for $20,000 at the end of its useful life. Gear has presented Widget with the following options: Buy the equipment. The equipment could be purchased for $200,000 in cash. All maintenance costs, which approximate $10,000 per year, would be paid by Widget. Lease the equipment. The equipment could be leased for a 7-year period for an annual lease payment of $40,000 with the first payment due immediately. All maintenance costs will be paid for by the Gear Inc. and the equipment will revert back to Gear at the end of the 7-year period. Assuming that a 10% interest rate properly reflects the time value of money in this situation and that all maintenance costs are paid at the end of each year, determine which option Widget should choose for its present value. Ignore taxes. A. $201.456.37 B. $214,210.43 C. $238,421.03 D. $270,000.00

As a high-profile executive of a Fortune 500 company, Elizabeth has been asked to accept an early retirement package. The company has offered her three enticing compensation packages to persuade her to retire. The packages are as follows: A lump sum of $500,000 to be paid immediately. An annuity of $50,000 per year for 25 years, with the first payment made immediately. An annuity of $100,000 per year for 10 years, with the first payment made on the date Elizabeth turns 65 years old (in 10 years). Assuming that Elizabeth can invest funds at a 8% rate, which alternative should she choose? A. Option 1: $500,000 cash payment B. Option 2: Annuity of $50,000 per year for 25 years, with the first payment made immediately C. Option 3: An annuity of $100,000 per year for 10 years, with the first payment made on the date Elizabeth turns 65 years old (in 10 years).
quizSrc.definition: B. Option 2: Annuity of $50,000 per year for 25 years, with the first payment made immediately

quizSrc.term: Which of the following is not a criteria for recognizing revenue under the accrual basis of accounting? A. The company has completed its obligation to the customer. B. The amount of revenue can be reasonably estimated. C. The customer has paid for the product or service. D. The company has received an order for the product or service.
quizSrc.definition: D. The company has received an order for the product or service.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following statements regarding performance obligations under US GAAP is true? A. A performance obligation must be satisfied over time B. A performance obligation must be satisfied at a specific point in time C. A performance obligation can only be satisfied when a customer has paid in full D. A performance obligation is a promise to transfer a distinct good or service to a customer
quizSrc.definition: D. A performance obligation is a promise to transfer a distinct good or service to a customer

quizSrc.term: Which of the following journal entries correctly represents revenue recognition under USGAAP? A. Debit Deferred Revenue $10,000, credit Accounts Receivable $10,000 B. Debit Deferred Revenue $10,000, credit Cash $10,000 C. Debit Accounts Receivable $10,000, credit Revenue $10,000 D. Debit Revenue $10,000, credit Accounts Receivable $10,000
quizSrc.definition: C. Debit Accounts Receivable $10,000, credit Revenue $10,000

quizSrc.term: In the realm of commerce and contractual obligations, Dunne Co. has crafted an agreement comprising two remarkable performance obligations. Behold Product A, with a majestic stand-alone selling price of $150, and Product B, graced with a stand-alone selling price of $300. Now, witness the grandeur of their combined offering, shimmering at a total price of $360. Enveloped in this transaction lies the question: What portion of this splendid price shall be artfully allocated to the performance obligation for delivering Product A? A. $90 B. $120 C. $150 D. $ 60

The prestigious Cora Co. has inked a wondrous agreement in contractual marvels and corporate dealings, graced with two distinct performance obligations. Its standalone selling price remains shrouded in mystery for the enigmatic Product A, impervious to estimation. Behold Product B, with an awe-inspiring standalone selling price of $250. Now, set your gaze upon the total of their combined offering, a price of $360. Amidst this transaction, the inquiry arises: What portion of this price shall be allocated to the performance obligation for delivering Product B? A. $360 B. $610 C. $250 D. $110

Dynamo Electronics, a renowned electronics manufacturer, has introduced an exclusive promotion for its cutting-edge tablet device - an extended service plan at a reduced rate. The standard price of the service plan is $400, but Dynamo is offering it for $320 when purchased with the tablet. They estimate a 60% likelihood that customers will opt for the discounted extended service plan when buying the tablet. Assuming Dynamo sells 500 tablets with the extended service plan discount offer, what would be the total stand-alone selling price allocated to the extended service plan option in these 500 tablet contracts? A. $24,000 B. $48,000 C. $96,000 D. $120,000

Amazonia Solutions operates a platform that connects stores around the world. When the customer tries to find a product online on Amazonia Solutions, and there isn’t that product, Amazonia connects the customer with other associated stores and receives a 15% fee commission from the other stores for facilitating the connection. One customer went through Amazonia and agreed to pay $8,000 for a product in Unga Bunga Store. How would this transaction be reflected on Amazonia Solutions’ income statement? A. Revenue of $8,000 B. Revenue of $8,000 and cost of services of $6,800 C. Revenue of $1,200 D. Revenue of $9,200 and cost of services of $8,000

Meet Harmony, an upscale boutique offering elegant gift cards redeemable for luxurious products. In 2025, Harmony sold $4,500,000 worth of gift cards, and customers redeemed $4,000,000 of the gift cards for their desired items. By December 31, 2025, Harmony determined that $250,000 of the remaining gift cards would unlikely be redeemed. How much gift card revenue should Harmony recognize in 2025? A. $4,750,000 B. $4,000,000 C. $4,500,000 D. $4,250,000

ABC Company enters into a consignment arrangement with XYZ Corporation to sell its products. The products are delivered to XYZ's premises, but ownership remains with ABC until the products are sold to end customers. Which of the following statements regarding revenue recognition in this consignment arrangement is correct? A. ABC Company recognizes revenue when the products are delivered to XYZ Corporation's premises. B. ABC Company recognizes revenue when the products are sold to end customers. C. ABC Company recognizes revenue when the consignment arrangement is established. D. ABC Company does not recognize revenue until ownership of the products transfers to XYZ Corporation.
quizSrc.definition: B. ABC Company recognizes revenue when the products are sold to end customers.

quizSrc.term: XYZ Corporation sold a product to a customer for $10,000. The customer has the option to pay the full amount upfront or make equal annual payments over a period of three years. XYZ Corporation uses a discount rate of 8% to assess the time value of money. Which of the following statements regarding revenue recognition and time value of money is correct? A. XYZ Corporation recognizes the full $10,000 as revenue upfront, regardless of the payment option chosen by the customer. B. XYZ Corporation recognizes revenue based on the total discounted cash flows, considering the time value of money. C. XYZ Corporation recognizes revenue evenly over the three-year period, regardless of the payment option chosen by the customer. D. XYZ Corporation does not recognize revenue until the customer makes the final payment.
quizSrc.definition: B. XYZ Corporation recognizes revenue based on the total discounted cash flows, considering the time value of money.

quizSrc.term: Company ABC enters into a long-term construction contract with a customer. The contract is expected to span over three years and has a total contract value of $1,000,000. According to US GAAP guidelines, which of the following methods is most commonly used for revenue recognition in long-term contracts? A. Completed Contract Method B. Percentage-of-Completion Method C. Installment Method D. Cost Recovery Method

Under US GAAP, how is Construction in Progress (CIP) typically classified and reported for long-term contracts? A. CIP is classified as a long-term liability on the balance sheet. B. CIP is classified as an intangible asset on the balance sheet. C. CIP is classified as a long-term asset on the balance sheet. D. CIP is classified as a current liability on the balance sheet.
quizSrc.definition: C. CIP is classified as a long-term asset on the balance sheet.

quizSrc.term: Company XYZ is engaged in a long-term construction project and has a contract with a customer for $2,000,000. As of the end of the reporting period, the company has billed the customer for $1,200,000 based on the progress of the project. According to US GAAP, how should the progress billings be reported in the financial statements? A. Recognize the $1,200,000 as a contra asset CIP account based on the percentage of completion method. B. Recognize the full $1,200,000 as revenue in the current reporting period. C. Recognize the full $1,200,000 as a liability in the current reporting period. D. Recognize a portion of the $1,200,000 as revenue based on the percentage of completion method.
quizSrc.definition: A. Recognize the $1,200,000 as a contra asset CIP account based on the percentage of completion method.

quizSrc.term: In the construction industry, Kyle Engineering Inc. has secured a contract to build a state-of-the-art bridge at a contracted price of $18,000,000. The company follows the percentage-of-completion method to recognize revenue over time. Here are the project updates: Cost Incurred 2023 $4,500,000 2024 $1,500,000 2025 $3,750,000 Estimated Additional Cost to Complete 2023 $9,000,000 2024 $6,750,000 2025 $0 What is the company’s percentage completion in 2023? A. 10% B. 20% C. 33% D. 50%

In the construction industry, Kyle Engineering Inc. has secured a contract to build a state-of-the-art bridge at a contracted price of $18,000,000. The company follows the percentage-of-completion method to recognize revenue over time. Here are the project updates: Cost Incurred 2023 $4,500,000 2024 $1,500,000 2025 $3,750,000 Estimated Additional Cost to Complete 2023 $9,000,000 2024 $6,750,000 2025 $0 What is the company’s revenue recognized in 2023? A. $1,800,000 B. $3,000,000 C. $6,000,000 D. $12,000,000

In the construction industry, Kyle Engineering Inc. has secured a contract to build a state-of-the-art bridge at a contracted price of $18,000,000. The company follows the percentage-of-completion method to recognize revenue over time. Here are the project updates: Cost Incurred 2023 $4,500,000 2024 $7,500,000 2025 $7,500,000 Estimated Additional Cost to Complete 2023 $9,000,000 2024 $6,750,000 2025 $0 What is the company’s profit/(loss) recognized in 2024? A. $5,520,000 B. $6,000,000 C. ($2,250,000) D. ($750,000)

Under US GAAP, how are losses for long-term contracts recognized? A. Losses are recognized only upon completion of the contract. B. Losses are not recognized for long-term contracts in US GAAP. C. Losses are recognized proportionally over the life of the contract based on the percentage of completion. D. Losses are recognized immediately when identified, regardless of the stage of completion.
quizSrc.definition: D. Losses are recognized immediately when identified, regardless of the stage of completion.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following statements describes a difference between revenue recognition in US GAAP and IFRS? A. US GAAP recognizes revenue when it is realized or realizable, while IFRS recognizes revenue when it is probable and can be reliably measured. B. US GAAP requires revenue to be recognized at the point of delivery, while IFRS allows for revenue recognition over time. C. US GAAP and IFRS have identical revenue recognition principles and requirements. D. US GAAP allows for more flexibility in revenue recognition timing compared to IFRS.
quizSrc.definition: A. US GAAP recognizes revenue when it is realized or realizable, while IFRS recognizes revenue when it is probable and can be reliably measured.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following statements accurately describes the difference between revenue recognition at a certain point in time and revenue recognition over a period of time? A. Revenue recognition at a certain point in time and revenue recognition over a period of time are the same concepts and do not have any differences. B. Revenue recognition at a certain point in time recognizes revenue based on the transfer of control, while revenue recognition over a period of time recognizes revenue when cash is received. C. Revenue recognition at a certain point in time recognizes revenue when cash is received, while revenue recognition over a period of time recognizes revenue when the right to receive payment is established. D. Revenue recognition at a certain point in time recognizes revenue when goods or services are delivered, while revenue recognition over a period of time recognizes revenue proportionally over the duration of a long-term contract.
quizSrc.definition: D. Revenue recognition at a certain point in time recognizes revenue when goods or services are delivered, while revenue recognition over a period of time recognizes revenue proportionally over the duration of a long-term contract.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following is not true about revenue recognition with respect to long-term construction contracts under US GAAP? A. Long-term construction contracts are often viewed as having multiple performance obligations due to the inclusion of different types of goods or services for each project. B. Long-term construction contracts often satisfy the criteria for recognizing revenue over time. C. Long-term construction contracts require accounting for construction in progress as well as billings to customers. D. Long-term construction contracts have multiple performance obligations as the goods or services are separately identifiable.
quizSrc.definition: D. Long-term construction contracts have multiple performance obligations as the goods or services are separately identifiable.

quizSrc.term: On December 31, 2023, the trial balance of Gardenia Corporation shows the following items: • Currency and coins: $1,000 • Balance in checking account: $4,500 • Customer checks waiting to be deposited: $2,300 • Marketable equity securities: $12,000 • Commercial paper, purchased on 11/1/2023, mature on 1/30/2024: $6,500 • Treasury bills, purchased on 12/1/2023, mature on 3/31/2024: $7,800 What amount will Gardenia Corporation report as cash and cash equivalents on its year-end balance sheet? A. $14,800 B. $22,100 C. $14,300 D. A) $7,800

The following information relates to Halloran Co.'s accounts receivable for 2024: Accounts receivable balance, 1/1/2024 $975,000 Credit sales for 2024 $4,500,000 Accounts receivable written off during 2024 $80,000 Collections from customers during 2024 $ 4,100,000 Allowance for uncollectible accounts balance, 12/31/2024 $350,000 What amount should Halloran report for accounts receivable, before allowances, on December 31, 2024? A. $1,065,000 B. $1,210,00 C. $1,295,000 D. $945,000

Which of the following statements accurately describes the difference between cash and accounts receivable? A. Cash represents the amount of money owed by customers, while accounts receivable represents actual physical currency. B. Cash and accounts receivable are interchangeable terms used to describe money owed by customers. C. Cash refers to the amount of money owed to suppliers, while accounts receivable refers to funds available for immediate use. D. Cash is a type of asset that includes physical money and funds available in bank accounts, while accounts receivable refers to money owed by customers for goods or services.
quizSrc.definition: D. Cash is a type of asset that includes physical money and funds available in bank accounts, while accounts receivable refers to money owed by customers for goods or services.

quizSrc.term: On August 31 of the current year, Iracema sold equipment to a customer for $10,000 with credit terms 2/10, n/30. Iracema uses the gross method of accounting for sales discounts. What is the correct entry for this company on August 31? A. Accounts receivable 10,000 Sales discounts 200 Sales 9,800 B. Dr - Accounts receivable 9,800 Dr - Sales discounts 200 Cr - Sales 10,000 C. Dr - Accounts receivable 9,800 Cr - Sales 9,800 D. Dr - Accounts receivable 10,000 Cr - Sales 10,000
quizSrc.definition: D. Dr - Accounts receivable 10,000 Cr - Sales 10,000

quizSrc.term: On August 31 of the current year, Iracema sold equipment to a customer for $10,000 with credit terms 2/10, n/30. Iracema uses the net method of accounting for sales discounts. What is the correct entry for this company on August 31? A. Accounts receivable 10,000 Sales discounts 200 Sales 9,800 B. Dr - Accounts receivable 9,800 Cr - Sales 9,800 C. Dr - Accounts receivable 9,800 Dr - Sales discounts 200 Cr - Sales 10,000 D. Dr - Accounts receivable 10,000 Cr - Sales 10,000
quizSrc.definition: B. Dr - Accounts receivable 9,800 Cr - Sales 9,800

quizSrc.term: In 2024, Ashbaugh Industries had sales totaling $1,500,000 and returns totaling $40,000. Returns are recorded as they occur, and additional estimated returns are accrued at the end of the period. The cost of goods sold for the year was $1,050,000, 70% of the total sales. Based on the company's estimation, 4% of all sales are expected to be returned. What would be the necessary year-end adjusting journal entry to account for the anticipated sales returns? A. Debit to refund liability for $20,000 B. Debit to cost of goods sold for $20,000 C. Credit to refund liability for $20,000 D. Credit to sales returns for $20,000
quizSrc.definition: C. Credit to refund liability for $20,000

quizSrc.term: Which of the following statements accurately describes the differences between the direct write-off method and the allowance method for recording bad debt expense? A. The direct write-off method is based on estimates, while the allowance method relies on specific identification of uncollectible accounts. B. The direct write-off method matches expenses with revenues, while the allowance method violates the matching principle. C. The direct write-off method reduces accounts receivable directly, while the allowance method uses a contra-asset account. D. The direct write-off method is required for tax purposes, while the allowance method is used for financial reporting.
quizSrc.definition: C. The direct write-off method reduces accounts receivable directly, while the allowance method uses a contra-asset account.

quizSrc.term: On April 1, Cicero Assoc. sold equipment to King Kong and accepted a 4-month, $100,000, noninterest-bearing note as payment. At the time of the transaction, the prevailing interest rate for similar transactions was 10%. How much discount will Cicero Assoc. recognize at the beginning of the transaction? A. $0 B. $2,500 C. $5,000 D. $3,333.33

The receivable turnover ratio is a financial ratio that measures: A. The liquidity of a company's assets. B. The profitability of a company's investments in receivables. C. The efficiency of a company's collection process for accounts receivable. D. The leverage of a company's debt.
quizSrc.definition: C. The efficiency of a company's collection process for accounts receivable.

quizSrc.term: Fighting Illini conducts monthly bank reconciliations. At the end of May, the general ledger checking account displayed a balance of $1,700, while the bank statement reported a bank balance of $1,966. Outstanding checks amounted to $446, and deposits in transit were $35. The bank statement listed service charges of $40 and NSF (non-sufficient funds) checks totaling $105. What is the adjusted cash balance after reconciling? A. $1,647 B. $1,555 C. $1,078 D. $1,559
Which of the following is an example of a tangible fixed asset? A. Goodwill B. Copyright C. Land D. Brand reputation
Which of the following is an example of an intangible asset? A. Cash B. Building C. Inventory D. Patent

A construction firm had the following expenditures for the year: January 1 $125,000 February 1 $250,000 March 31 $750,000 August 31 $550,000 November 30 $150,000 The interest rate was 9.5%. What is the amount of interest that the firm capitalized for the year? A. $0 B. $105,688 C. $111,625 D. $106,875

A company purchased new machinery equipment for $180,000. The company paid cash for the equipment. Other costs associated with the equipment were: transportation costs, $10,000; sales tax paid $25,000; installation cost, $15,000 & annual maintenance costs of $7,500. The cost recorded for the equipment was: A. $205,000 B. $230,000 C. $237,500 D. $227,500

Below are financial details for a research laboratory. Salaries for lab research 1,250,000 Materials used in R&D projects 350,000 Purchase of equipment 550,000 Fees paid to outside contractors for R&D 400,000 Patent and other legal fees 95,000 Salaries for R&D performed for another firm 225,000 Equipment useful life 8 years Depreciation for the year 175,000 The equipment will be used for multiple R&D projects. What will R&D expense be for 2018? A. $2,725,000 B. $2,400,000 C. $2,000,000 D. $2,175,000

Which of the following is an example of an R&D cost? A. Routine ongoing efforts to refine, enrich, or otherwise improve on the qualities of an existing product B. Quality control during commercial production C. None of these answers is an example of an R&D cost D. Engineering follow-through on an early phase of commercial production
quizSrc.definition: C. None of these answers is an example of an R&D cost

quizSrc.term: Santos Co. exchanged land and $7,000 cash for equipment. The book value and the fair value of the land were $112,000 and $95,000, respectively. Assuming that the exchange has commercial substance, Santos would record equipment and a gain/(loss) on exchange of assets in the amounts of: A. Equipment Gain/(loss) $102,000 $(17,000) B. Equipment Gain/(loss) $88,000 $(24,000) C. Equipment Gain/(loss) $106,000 $(17,000) D. Equipment Gain/(loss) $102,000 $17,000

Gen Corp. acquired a manufacturing facility on five acres of land for a lump-sum price of $8,000,000. The building included used but functional equipment. Independent appraisals said the fair values were $4,500,000, $3,000,000, and $2,500,000 for the building, land, and equipment, respectively. The initial values of the building, land, and equipment would be: A. $3,600,000; $2,400,000; $2,000,000 B. $4,500,000; $3,000,000; $2,500,000 C. $4,500,000; $3,000,000; $500,000 D. $4,000,000; $2,500,000; $1,500,000

Goodwill is the excess of the purchase price of an acquired company over the: A. Book value of the acquired company. B. Sum of the fair values of the assets acquired. C. Fair value, only if higher than book value of the acquired company. D. Fair value of the net assets acquired.
quizSrc.definition: D. Fair value of the net assets acquired.

quizSrc.term: Under U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (US GAAP), what is an asset retirement obligation (ARO)? A. A reserve established to account for potential losses on financial investments. B. A liability that represents the estimated costs for the retirement of tangible fixed assets. C. A capital expenditure incurred for the acquisition of new assets. D. An intangible asset that represents the future benefits from the retirement of long-term assets.
quizSrc.definition: B. A liability that represents the estimated costs for the retirement of tangible fixed assets.

quizSrc.term: Archie Co. purchased a framing machine for $53,000 on January 1, 2024. The machine is expected to have a four-year life, with a residual value of $5,000 at the end of four years. Using the straight-line method, depreciation for 2024 and book value at December 31, 2024, would be: A. $13,000 and $36,000 respectively. B. $13,000 and $41,000 respectively. C. $12,000 and $36,000 respectively. D. $12,000 and $41,000 respectively.

A company purchased an asset on May 1, 2022, for $150,000 with an estimated three-year life and $10,000 residual value. The company uses units-of-production depreciation and expects the asset to produce 25,000 units. Calculate the gain or loss if the asset was sold on August 31, 2024, for $105,000. Actual production was: 2022 = 2,500 units; 2023 = 10,000 units; 2024 = 6,500 units. Which of the following options shows the correct gain or loss on the sale of the asset using the given information? A. Gain of $25,000 B. Loss of $25,000 C. Gain of $61,400 D. Loss of $61,400

Lucas Corp. owns equipment for which it paid $60 million. At the end of 2022, it had accumulated depreciation on the equipment of $25 million. Due to adverse economic conditions, Lucas’ management determined that it should assess whether an impairment loss should be recognized for the equipment. The estimated undiscounted future cash flows to be provided by the equipment total $45 million, and the equipment's fair value at that point is $35 million. Under these circumstances, Lucas: A. Should not recognize an impairment loss B. Should recognize an impairment loss of $5 million C. Should recognize an impairment loss of $15 million D. Should recognize an impairment loss of $10 million
quizSrc.definition: A. Should not recognize an impairment loss

quizSrc.term: Cora Co. purchased a piece of equipment for $450,000 on January 1, 2017, with a useful life of 15 years. The company uses straight-line depreciation for all of its assets. In 2019, this asset was depreciated for $25,000. What is the residual value of this piece of equipment? A. $50,000 B. $25,000 C. $75,000 D. $35,000

The fixed asset turnover ratio is a financial metric that measures: A. The level of debt a company has in relation to its fixed assets. B. The efficiency of a company in generating profits from its fixed assets. C. The accuracy of a company's fixed asset accounting records. D. The speed at which a company acquires new fixed assets.
quizSrc.definition: B. The efficiency of a company in generating profits from its fixed assets.

quizSrc.term: A machine that costs $200,000 has an expected service life of five years and a residual value of $20,000. Depreciation for the second year of the asset’s life using the double-declining-balance method is: A. $80,000 B. $72,000 C. $51,200 D. $48,000

Leek Corporation acquired a coal mine at a total cost of $25,000,000. The mine is expected to produce 50,000,000 tons of coal over its three-year useful life. During the first year of operations, 2,500,000 tons of coal were extracted. Depletion expense for the first year should be: A. None of the option is incorrect. B. $1,500,000 C. $1,250,000 D. $2,500,000

The Natal Corporation purchased equipment on January 1, 2024, at a cost of $180,000. The equipment had an estimated service life of 10 years and a residual value of $18,000. The company utilizes the straight-line depreciation method. However, in 2026, the estimate of the equipment's residual value was revised to zero. What is the revised depreciation expense for the equipment in 2026? A. $18,450 B. $17,676 C. $18,000 D. $16,875

The Galilee Company acquired equipment on January 1, 2024, for $200,000, with an estimated service life of 10 years and a residual value of $10,000. The company follows a fiscal year-end of December 31 and uses the double-declining balance (DDB) depreciation method. However, in 2026, the company switched to the straight-line depreciation method. What is the adjusting entry for depreciation expense in 2026? A. $14,750 B. $15,800 C. $19,750 D. $22,000

Assuming an asset is used evenly over a five-year service life, which method of depreciation will always result in the largest amount of depreciation in the first year? A. Units-of-production. B. Double-declining-balance. C. Sum-of-the-years'-digits. D. Straight-line.

Under U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (US GAAP), how are debt investments initially recognized and subsequently measured? A. Debt investments are initially recognized at amortized cost and subsequently measured at fair value. B. Debt investments are initially recognized at historical cost and subsequently measured at fair value or amortized cost. C. Debt investments are initially recognized at fair value and subsequently measured at fair value through other comprehensive income. D. Under U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (US GAAP), how are debt investments initially recognized and subsequently measured?
quizSrc.definition: B. Debt investments are initially recognized at historical cost and subsequently measured at fair value or amortized cost.

quizSrc.term: Which statements accurately describe a difference between debt and equity investments? A. Debt investments are classified as current assets, while equity investments are classified as long-term liabilities. B. Debt investments are reported at fair value, while equity investments are reported at historical cost. C. Debt investments represent ownership interests in a company, while equity investments represent loans made to a company. D. Debt investments provide a fixed rate of return, while equity investments provide potential for both income and capital appreciation.
quizSrc.definition: D. Debt investments provide a fixed rate of return, while equity investments provide potential for both income and capital appreciation.

quizSrc.term: In financial reporting, what is the key difference between the stated rate and the market rate? A. The stated rate always increases over time, while the market rate fluctuates based on economic conditions. B. The government sets the stated rate, while the market rate is determined by supply and demand. C. The stated rate is used to calculate the interest paid on a financial instrument, while the market rate reflects the prevailing interest rates in the market. D. The stated rate applies to debt instruments, while the market rate applies to equity instruments.
quizSrc.definition: C. The stated rate is used to calculate the interest paid on a financial instrument, while the market rate reflects the prevailing interest rates in the market.

quizSrc.term: Cema Inc. acquires bonds with a maturity value of $900,000 for $870,000. How would Cema record the purchase of these bonds in its journal entry? A. Credit of $30,000 to Premium on bond investment B. Debit of $30,000 to Premium on bond investment C. Debit of $30,000 to Discount on bond investment D. Credit of $30,000 to Discount on bond investment
quizSrc.definition: C. Debit of $30,000 to Discount on bond investment

quizSrc.term: On July 1, 2025, Beauty Hotels Corp. issued $600,000 of 10% bonds, dated July 1. The bonds mature in ten years, and interest is payable semiannually on June 30 and December 31. The market interest rate for bonds of similar risk and maturity is 12%. Joe, Inc. purchased the entire bond issue. On January 1, 2027, Titanic Corp. decided to sell its debt investment for $550,000, while the bonds had an amortized cost of $580,000. What would Titanic's journal entry to record the sale of the bonds include? A. Debit Cash for $550,000, Debit Loss on Sale of Investments for $50,000, Credit Investment - Bonds for $600,000, and Credit Discount on Bond Investment for $30,000. B. Debit Cash for $550,000, Debit Loss on Sale of Investments for $30,000, Debit Discount on Bond Investment for $20,000, and Credit Investment - Bonds for $600,000. C. Debit Loss on Sale of Investments for $30,000, Debit Cash for $550,000, Credit Investment - Bonds for $600,000, and Credit Discount on Bond Investment for $20,000. D. Debit Loss on Sale of Investments for $50,000, Debit Cash for $550,000, Credit Investment - Bonds for $600,000, and Credit Discount on Bond Investment for $30,000.
quizSrc.definition: B. Debit Cash for $550,000, Debit Loss on Sale of Investments for $30,000, Debit Discount on Bond Investment for $20,000, and Credit Investment - Bonds for $600,000.

quizSrc.term: What are the key differences between trading, available-for-sale, and held-to-maturity debt investments in US GAAP? A. The classification is based on the type of interest rate (fixed or variable), the investment's credit rating, and the investment's marketability. B. The classification is based on the investment's original cost, the market value at the reporting date, and the expected return on the investment. C. The classification is based on the investor's industry sector, the level of diversification in the investment portfolio, and the maturity of the investment. D. The classification is based on the investor's intent, the recognition of unrealized gains or losses, and the expected holding period.
quizSrc.definition: D. The classification is based on the investor's intent, the recognition of unrealized gains or losses, and the expected holding period.

quizSrc.term: Wenner Inc. purchased several investments in debt securities during 2024, its first year of operations. The following information pertains to these securities. The fluctuations in their fair values are not considered permanent. Held-to-Maturity Securities: Fair Value 12/31/2024 Fair Value 12/31/2025 Amortized Cost 12/31/2024 Amortized Cost 12/31/2025 Natal Co. Bonds $375,000 $400,000 $367,500 $360,000 Trading Securities: Fair Value 12/31/2024 Fair Value 12/31/2025 Cost Recife Co. Bonds $48,000 $59,500 $60,000 Fortaleza Inc. Bonds $47,000 $77,000 $39,000 Joao Pessoa Inc. Bonds $44,000 $38,500 $32,900 Available-for-Sale Securities: Fair Value 12/31/2024 Fair Value 12/31/2025 Cost Maceio Co. Bonds $130,500 $160,500 $140,000 What would be the balance in Wenner’s accumulated other comprehensive income with respect to these investments in its 12/31/2025 balance sheet (ignore taxes)? A. $55,100. B. None of the option is correct. C. $26,500. D. $10,400.

Chips Corporation bought 1,000 bonds of Curry Corporation in 2018 for $550 per bond, categorizing them as available-for-sale securities. The value of the Curry investment was $600 per bond on December 31, 2019, and $650 per bond on December 31, 2020. In 2021, Chips sold all the Curry bonds for $700 per bond. If Chips recognizes unrealized holding gains and losses until the sale, what would be the reclassification adjustment amount recorded by Chips upon the sale? A. A credit of $150,000. B. A debit of $150,000. C. A debit of $50,000. D. A debit of $200,000.

On January 1, Company X purchased bonds with a face value of $20,000 issued by Company Y. The stated rate of the bonds is 10% paid annually, and the bond matures in 4 years. The market rate for similar bonds is 12%. Company X has the intent of holding the bonds until maturity. What would Company X record as cash received for interests at the end of each year? A. $18,785 B. $3,037 C. $1,215 D. $2,000
Which of the following financial instruments is considered an equity security that investors would purchase? A. Treasury bill B. Notes C. Common stock D. Bonds

On January 1, 2024, Sampaio Corporation purchased 20% of the outstanding voting common stock of Lima Company for $600,000. The book value of the acquired shares was $575,000. The excess cost over book value is attributable to an intangible asset on Lima’s books that was undervalued and had a five-year useful life. For the year ended December 31, 2024, Lima reported a net income of $225,000 and paid cash dividends of $25,000. What is the carrying value of Sampaio’s investment in Lima on December 31, 2021? A. $600,000 B. $625,000 C. $635,000 D. $645,000

Which of the following is true regarding the fair value option for investments that are accounted for using the equity method: A. The fair value option requires additional disclosures B. The election must be made for all of the firm’s investments that are accounted for using the equity method C. The fair value option is an irrevocable election D. Unrealized gains and losses are included in other comprehensive income
quizSrc.definition: C. The fair value option is an irrevocable election

quizSrc.term: On July 1, 2024, Lima Corporation acquired 40% of the shares of Alice Company. Lima paid $3,100,000 for the investment, which equals 40% of the book value of identifiable net assets on Alice’s balance sheet. Alice recognized net income of $1,000,000 for 2024 and paid its shareholders $150,000 in dividends each quarter. After all closing entries are made, Lima’s "Investment in Alice Company" account would have a balance of: A. $3,080,000 B. $3,000,000 C. $3,200,000 D. $3,180,000

What are the key differences between the fair value through net income (FVTNI) and equity method (EM) for equity investments in US GAAP? A. The classification is based on the investor's level of control, the recognition of dividends received, and the measurement of the investment at cost. B. The classification is based on the investor's level of control, the recognition of unrealized gains or losses, and the measurement of the investment at fair value. C. The classification is based on the investor's level of ownership, the investor's intent, and the equity recognition in the investee's income. D. The classification is based on the investor's level of ownership, the recognition of dividends received, and the measurement of the investment at fair value.
quizSrc.definition: D. The classification is based on the investor's level of ownership, the recognition of dividends received, and the measurement of the investment at fair value.

quizSrc.term: Under US GAAP, which of the following statements regarding fair value through net income (FVTNI) classification for equity investments is true? A. The FVTNI classification requires the equity investment to be measured at fair value with changes recorded in the income statement. B. The FVTNI classification allows for the recognition of dividends received from the equity investment. C. The FVTNI classification is primarily used for long-term investments held by financial institutions. D. The FVTNI classification is used when the investor has significant influence over the investee.
quizSrc.definition: A. The FVTNI classification requires the equity investment to be measured at fair value with changes recorded in the income statement.

quizSrc.term: The equity method is used when an investor can't control, but can exercise significant influence over the operating and financial policies of the investee. We presume, in the absence of evidence to the contrary, that this is so if: A. The investor classifies the investment as available-for-sale. B. The investor owns between 20% and 50% of the investee's voting shares. C. The investor classifies the investment as held-to-maturity. D. The investor owns between 51% or more of the investee's voting shares.
quizSrc.definition: B. The investor owns between 20% and 50% of the investee's voting shares.

quizSrc.term: If Brazil Company exercises significant influence over Argentina Company and owns 40% of its common stock, then Brazil Company: A. Would increase its investment account when Argentina Company declares dividends. B. Would record 40% of the net income of Argentina Company as investment income each year. C. Would increase its investment account when recording 40% of dividends paid. D. Would record dividends received from Argentina Company as investment revenue.
quizSrc.definition: B. Would record 40% of the net income of Argentina Company as investment income each year.

quizSrc.term: Assume Apple buys 30% of Microsoft’s common stock for $200 million on January 1, 2023. Microsoft has $350 million in net income for 2023, declares a dividend of $10 million in 2023, and its stock price increases by 20% during 2023. What value would Apple show on its balance sheet at 12/31/2023 for its investment in Microsoft? A. $308,000,000 B. $200,000,000 C. $240,000,000 D. $302,000,000

Lolla Corporation purchased 12% of Kyle Corporation's common stock on January 1st, 2023, for $50,000. Kyle Corporation reported net incomes of $10,000 and $50,000 for the years ending December 31, 2023, and December 31, 2024, respectively. In 2024, Kyle Corporation declared and paid a dividend of $60,000, while no dividends were declared in 2023. The fair value of Lolla's investment in Kyle Corporation's stock increased to $70,000 on December 31, 2024. How much income should Lolla Corporation report in its 2024 income statement from this investment? A. $26,000. B. $27,200. C. $7,200. D. $20,000.

Under US GAAP, which of the following statements is true regarding the equity method for equity investments? A. The equity method requires the equity investment to be measured at fair value with changes recorded in the income statement. B. The equity method is primarily used for short-term investments held by financial institutions. C. The equity method is used when the investor has significant influence over the investee. D. The equity method allows for the recognition of dividends received from the equity investment.
quizSrc.definition: C. The equity method is used when the investor has significant influence over the investee.

quizSrc.term: The Constance Corporation's inventory on December 31, 2021, was $125,000 (at cost) based on a physical count of inventory on hand, before any necessary adjustment for the following: • Merchandise costing $51,000, shipped f.o.b. shipping point from a vendor on December 27, 2021, was received by Constance on January 5, 2022. • Merchandise costing $45,000 was shipped to a customer f.o.b. shipping point on December 28, 2021, arrived at the customer's location on January 6, 2022. • Merchandise costing $12,000 was being held on hand for Jess Company on consignment. • Estimated sales returns are 10% of annual sales. Sales revenue was $550,000 with a gross profit ratio of 25%. What amount should Constance Corporation report as inventory in its December 31, 2021, balance sheet? A. $ 250,250 B. None of these answer choices are correct. C. $ 205,250 D. $ 232,250

quizSrc.term: When inventory costs are increasing: A. FIFO produces higher COGS, lower current Net Income, and higher Ending Inventory. B. FIFO produces lower COGS, higher current Net Income, and lower Ending Inventory. C. FIFO produces higher COGS, lower current Net Income, and lower Ending Inventory. D. FIFO produces lower COGS, higher current Net Income, and higher Ending Inventory.
quizSrc.definition: D. FIFO produces lower COGS, higher current Net Income, and higher Ending Inventory.

quizSrc.term: ABC Company had net sales of $500,000 and cost of goods sold (COGS) of $350,000 for the year. If operating expenses were $100,000, what is the gross profit ratio for ABC Company? A. 45% B. 40% C. 30% D. 35%

ABC Company holds a certain inventory item with a cost of $10,000. Due to a decline in market demand and changes in technology, its selling price equals $8,000, and the cost to sell equals $500. If ABC Company follows the Lower of Cost or Net Realizable Value (LCNRV) rule in accordance with U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (U.S. GAAP), what value should ABC report the inventory item on their financial statements? Which of the following is the correct formula used to determine the cost-to-retail percentage? A. Net sales at cost divided by net sales at retail B. Net purchases at cost divided by net purchases at retail C. Goods available for sale at cost divided by goods available for sale at retail D. Cost of goods sold at cost divided by cost of goods sold at retail
quizSrc.definition: C. Goods available for sale at cost divided by goods available for sale at retail

A. $29,864 B. $33,428 C. $39,429 D. $45,000

ABC Company discovered an inventory error in its records. The company's physical count of inventory revealed a quantity of 500 units, while the recorded quantity in the books was 450 units. The cost per unit of inventory is $20. What is the impact of this inventory error on the company's reported inventory value? A. Understatement of $500 B. Overstatement of $1,000 C. Understatement of $1,000 D. Overstatement of $500

ABC Company has been using the Average Cost inventory method for several years but is considering changing to the LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) method. Which of the following statements is a potential advantage of switching to the LIFO method? A. LIFO simplifies recordkeeping and cost calculations. B. LIFO results in lower taxable income during periods of rising prices. C. LIFO provides a better reflection of the physical flow of inventory. D. LIFO enhances financial statement comparability across different companies.
quizSrc.definition: B. LIFO results in lower taxable income during periods of rising prices.

quizSrc.term: XYZ Corporation has been using the First-In, First-Out (FIFO) method to value its inventory for several years but is considering changing to the Weighted Average Cost method. Which of the following statements is a potential advantage of switching to the Weighted Average Cost method? A. Weighted Average Cost enhances financial statement comparability across different companies. B. Weighted Average Cost provides a better reflection of the physical flow of inventory. C. Weighted Average Cost simplifies recordkeeping and cost calculations. D. Weighted Average Cost results in lower taxable income during periods of falling prices.
quizSrc.definition: C. Weighted Average Cost simplifies recordkeeping and cost calculations.

quizSrc.term: When comparing International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) and Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) regarding the valuation of inventories, which of the following statements is true? A. U.S. GAAP permits the use of LIFO, while IFRS prohibits the use of LIFO. B. Both IFRS and U.S. GAAP explicitly state the valuation method to be used for inventories. C. IFRS allows the use of either LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) or FIFO (First-In, First-Out) methods, while U.S. GAAP only permits the use of FIFO. D. Both IFRS and US GAAP require the use of the same inventory valuation methods.
quizSrc.definition: A. U.S. GAAP permits the use of LIFO, while IFRS prohibits the use of LIFO.

quizSrc.term: An interest-bearing note payable on the balance sheet of CMA Co. will require the following payments: A. Stated face amount at issuance along with periodic interest payments determined by stated interest rate. B. Stated face amount at maturity along with periodic interest payments determined by stated interest rate. C. Stated face amount at maturity along with periodic interest payments determined by the effective interest rate. D. Stated face amount at the beginning along with periodic interest payments determined by LIBOR + state’s interest rate.
quizSrc.definition: B. Stated face amount at maturity along with periodic interest payments determined by stated interest rate.

quizSrc.term: Which of the following statements accurately describes an installment bond? A. An installment bond does not require periodic payments of principal and interest. B. An installment bond only requires periodic payments of interest, not principal. C. An installment bond involves periodic payments of both principal and interest on the loan. D. An installment bond requires the same payment amount for each period, with no variation in principal and interest allocation.
quizSrc.definition: C. An installment bond involves periodic payments of both principal and interest on the loan.

Which one of the following statements is not correct? A. Long-term debt that is callable by the lender in the upcoming year will not be classified as a current liability if the debt is not expected to be called. B. Current liabilities could include estimated amounts. C. The ability and intent to refinance short-term liabilities on a long-term basis influence the classification of the short-term liabilities. D. quizSrc.definition: A. Long-term debt that is callable by the lender in the upcoming year will not be classified as a current liability if the debt is not expected to be called.

How does the classification of short-term debt differ between US GAAP and IFRS? A. Short-term debt classification is the same under both US GAAP and IFRS. B. US GAAP allows short-term debt to be classified as noncurrent if the intent and ability to refinance is demonstrated before the financial statement issuance. IFRS allows short-term debt to be classified as noncurrent if the intent and ability to refinance is demonstrated before the period-end date. C. Both US GAAP and IFRS both allow short-term debt to be classified as noncurrent if the intent and ability to refinance is demonstrated before the financial statement issuance. D. US GAAP allows short-term debt to be classified as noncurrent if the intent and ability to refinance is demonstrated before the period-end date. IFRS allows short-term debt to be classified as noncurrent if the intent and ability to refinance is demonstrated before the financial statement issuance.
quizSrc.definition: B. US GAAP allows short-term debt to be classified as noncurrent if the intent and ability to refinance is demonstrated before the financial statement issuance. IFRS allows short-term debt to be classified as noncurrent if the intent and ability to refinance is demonstrated before the period-end date.

The total lease related revenue in 2022 for Noel Corporation (lessor) is: A. $29,401 B. $34,497 C. $40,000 D. $10,599

A. 4% B. 6% C. 17% D. 8%
